title: Cirrus: Bump Fedora to release 35
body:
#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Update the "Fedora" VM used in testing to version `35` (beta), and the "Prior Fedora" version to `34`.  Stop testing with Fedora `33`.  Also resolve a `TODO` in setup - the needed packages are now bundled into the images.

***NOTE:*** The Fedora 35 image is now based on a BTRFS root filesystem.

#### How to verify it

CI will pass

#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes:

None

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

Depends on: #11955 and #11979 and ##12110 and #12121 or #12120 and #12060 and #12162 and #12342 and #12343 and https://github.com/containers/automation_images/pull/93

comments.body: Sorry, forgot to mark this as WIP/Draft.  There are some major challenges that need solving: Namely the kernel shouldn't panic when compiling podman :cry:
@lsm5 [I'm getting test-rpm build errors on the new F35 VM.](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5781764963565568?logs=main#L361-L371)  Do you know how to fix these?

[For `int podman fedora-35` root and rootless](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/6415083661164544), the tests are hanging.  Both appear to be [getting stuck on a `checkpoint` test (log)](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5992871196098560/ginkgo_node_logs/test/e2e/ginkgo-node-3.log).

Edit: My mistake, not "rootless" but "container" integration.
@adrianreber ping - any insights on the checkpoint hang?

> @adrianreber ping - any insights on the checkpoint hang?

rootless tests should not run any checkpoint tests at all. The root test hangs while trying to establish a TCP connection to the container. I can run it locally on f35 tomorrow to see if I can reproduce it.

Not sure why it should hang with f35 right now:
```
                // Open a network connection to the redis server via initial port mapping
                conn, err := net.Dial("tcp", "localhost:1234")
                if err != nil {
                        os.Exit(1)
                }
                conn.Close()
```

Most checkpoint tests seem to be running. Just as an FYI, if all checkpoint tests start to fail: CRIU needs something like `chattr +C /var/lib/containers` to work with BTRFS.

> CRIU needs something like chattr +C /var/lib/containers to work with BTRFS.

BTRFS will be the stock/default for F35 VMs, so should we preemptively change that at the packaging level?  Or is that just a caution / might happen warning?

> @lsm5 [I'm getting test-rpm build errors on the new F35 VM.](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5781764963565568?logs=main#L361-L371) Do you know how to fix these?

disable debuginfo in specfile, set `%global debug_package %{nil}` . I thought this was disabled already in a prior PR from @Luap99 . But maybe not?

> > CRIU needs something like chattr +C /var/lib/containers to work with BTRFS.
> 
> BTRFS will be the stock/default for F35 VMs, so should we preemptively change that at the packaging level?  Or is that just a caution / might happen warning?

Just that it might happen. If it works without the chattr, you can leave it as it is.

> disable debuginfo in specfile, set `%global debug_package %{nil}` . I thought this was disabled already in a prior PR from @Luap99 . But maybe not?

My PR is not merged yet (#11565).
Okay great, thanks @lsm5 and @Luap99, and no rush merging that, there are plenty of other issues to address in this PR in the meantime.

> I can run it locally on f35 tomorrow to see if I can reproduce it.

@adrianreber were you able to reproduce the hang at all?
No, unfortunately not. It just works in my VM.
Darn.

New images: I see a problem on F34, where `/` diskspace is not being expanded as it should.  Oddly, it appears that F35 BTRFS `/` ***is*** being expanded.  Both are experiencing test failures/hangs :cry:

Second time in a row [the `podman checkpoint and restore container with different port mappings` hangs](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/4833004993380352/ginkgo_node_logs/test/e2e/ginkgo-node-1.log) on F35.  I'm going to try and instrument the code to see where the hang is happening.

@adrianreber I[ instrumented the test with a bunch of `fmt.Printf()`](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/9744f900217bcdaffcc5ecdd40a60351e49de078#diff-e9325abde05ad8a2d00b6073f229cb4d2599abdf6965e03721f9c6ea216380a2R950) and ran it manually.  It seems to be hanging on the `conn, err := net.Dial("tcp", "localhost:1234")`.  Should that be wrapped in some kind of timeout, and shouldn't it be connecting to port `6379`?

> @adrianreber I[ instrumented the test with a bunch of `fmt.Printf()`](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/9744f900217bcdaffcc5ecdd40a60351e49de078#diff-e9325abde05ad8a2d00b6073f229cb4d2599abdf6965e03721f9c6ea216380a2R950) and ran it manually. It seems to be hanging on the `conn, err := net.Dial("tcp", "localhost:1234")`. Should that be wrapped in some kind of timeout, and shouldn't it be connecting to port `6379`?

The timeout would be a good idea. The port is correct with 1234 because that test is trying to handle different port mappings after restore. I am confused, however, I do not see the broken test being run. There is still a timeout but I am also not sure why.

Can you also try a change like this (in addition to your timeout change):
```
                conn, err := net.Dial("tcp", "localhost:1234")
-               if err != nil {
-                       os.Exit(1)
-               }
+               Expect(err).To(BeNil())
```
The `Exit(1)` is probably a bad idea anyway. I also had a look at the audit log but it does not seem to be a SELinux denial.
@cevich Thanks to beaker I am able to reproduce. I will provide a fix.

Fantastic, thanks @adrianreber .  For the future...if you ever need to go hands-on with a VM 99.999% identical to CI, just poke me on IRC.  We have a script that that makes this super easy, I can just manually plop your ssh key into it.

@adrianreber as is the nature of these things, fixing the hang seems to have revealed [another one in `podman checkpoint container with established tcp connections` (useless log)](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5956019621724160/ginkgo_node_logs/test/e2e/ginkgo-node-1.log).  I'll poke around and see if I can duct-tape a similar fix in with my white-belt golang kung-fu.  But I'm not hopeful.  Let me know if you need a VM to debug.

> @adrianreber as is the nature of these things, fixing the hang seems to have revealed [another one in `podman checkpoint container with established tcp connections` (useless log)](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5956019621724160/ginkgo_node_logs/test/e2e/ginkgo-node-1.log). I'll poke around and see if I can duct-tape a similar fix in with my white-belt golang kung-fu. But I'm not hopeful. Let me know if you need a VM to debug.

This test probably needs the same fix. Wait for container to be ready and use random port numbers.

> This test probably needs the same fix.

Okay thanks for the advice.  I'll give it a whirl and poke you if I run into trouble.
@cevich looks like a lot of journald issues reading the log.  @nalind PTAL

This one seems common across multiple tasks:

[+1812s] [Fail] Podman systemd [It] podman run container with systemd PID1 
[+1812s] /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/test/e2e/systemd_test.go:110

Ref: [Logs](https://cirrus-ci.com/build/5579852192415744)
[Next run logs w/ more `Podman systemd [It] podman run container with systemd PID1` failures](https://cirrus-ci.com/build/5619002933248000) among a scattering of others.

(Same run) int podman fedora-35 root container - [podman logs test](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/5323107402514432/html/int-podman-fedora-35-root-container.log.html#t--podman-create---mount-flag-with-multiple-mounts--1) has an interesting error: `Error: initial journal cursor: failed to get cursor: cannot assign requested address`

Edit: Interestingly, after looking through more 'in container' logs, this seems to be behind most/all failures there.

Edit: Annotated logs for `int podman fedora-35 root host` [podman run container with systemd PID1](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/5956019621724160/html/int-podman-fedora-35-root-host.log.html).  Error:

```
         Running: podman [options] exec -t -i testSystemd systemctl status --no-pager
         System has not been booted with systemd as init system (PID 1). Can't operate.
         Failed to connect to bus: Host is down
```
The log reader appears to be timing out while waiting for the container's first journal entry to show up in the journal.  Do we have information about how long the test is taking to run?

> Do we have information about how long the test is taking to run?

Possibly/probably.  Are you looking for overall runtime or specific tests? (sorry, I'm a scatter-brain - I can't keep track of what problem/context we're talking about)

> > Do we have information about how long the test is taking to run?
> 
> Possibly/probably. Are you looking for overall runtime or specific tests? (sorry, I'm a scatter-brain - I can't keep track of what problem/context we're talking about)

The specific journal-related tests that are failing.  We try to obtain a cursor, and if we fail to do so, we wait 100ms and try again, twice.  If it's a matter of needing to wait longer so that a test running in CI will be able to succeed there, that's easy.  Anything else would require more debugging.

Ahh, okay, thanks for the context.  The VMs are slow, I can say that with certainty :grin: I'm doubting we have timing details @ that level of granularity.  I think we only measure execution time of the test-function itself.  However, it is a consistent test and environment that reproduces it, so I could craft up a setup to do that faster, and we can instrument the single test with more granular timing info...

...thinking more.  This is ***only*** affecting F35, when tests run inside a container.  Those same tests are all passing on F34.  `int podman fedora-35 root container` [Logs with freshly built VM images.](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/5209936826204160/html/int-podman-fedora-35-root-container.log.html).  Let me see if I can whip up a simple reproducer using `hack/get_ci_vm.sh`...

.. @nalind this issue readily reproduces so I opened https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/12029

Timing does not matter, there was maybe 3-minutes between commands listed in that issue (as I read, and copy-pasted parts of them from the logs).

@mtrmac PTAL [F35 image signing test failure (annotated log)](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/6614426968129536/html/int-podman-fedora-35-root-host.log.html#t--podman-image-trust-show---json--1).  I can file an issue if you like, but it seems it could be a simple test-fix?  I simply don't know enough about this stuff to do it the right way.

@cevich From a quick skim the `podman image trust show --json` and `podman image trust show --raw` tests, which verify form of the output, assume what the surrounding environment (`/etc/containers/policy.json`) is (unless there are some environment overrides I’m not aware of; I can’t find anything of that kind).

Did the default contents of `/etc/containers/policy.json` change between F34 to F35 to add `registry.redhat.io` and `r.a.rh.com` policies enforcing signatures? If not, I’d need to dig in deeper.

If this is explained by the distribution default config changing, there are about three ways to handle it:
- Update the tests to expect the new system-wide default policy contents (but the tests would start failing on other systems / versions)
- Modify the system-wide OS configuration to have a specific `policy.json` content, e.g. the old one (or better, the new one and update the tests, which gets us more coverage); probably not something tests should require
- Almost certainly the best option: modify the tests to be similar to the `podman image trust show` test above, which passes a `--policypath` option pointing to a test-specific policy file with fixed contents, instead of making assumptions about the surrounding environment. (A quick fix: Make that file with F34-like contents. A better fix: Make that file with more complex contents, closer to F35, and adjust expected output to match that. E.g. the existing `https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/3c1d9910afaee21050ecbf058b5eddffc74c7abe/test/policy.json` is a pretty good example policy, and using that for all the `podman image trust show` tests would be elegantly consistent.)

> Did the default contents of /etc/containers/policy.json change between F34 to F35

I believe this default file would have come from [containers-common-1-32.fc35-noarch (log)](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/6614426968129536?logs=package_versions#L6).  I looked, and didn't see any place where we replace this file at runtime (unless that test/suite does it - I didn't look).

> If this is explained by the distribution default config changing, there are about three ways to handle it:
>
> * Update the tests to expect the new system-wide default policy contents (but the tests would start failing on other systems / versions)

Damn.

> * Modify the system-wide OS configuration to have a specific policy.json content, e.g. the old one (or better, the new one and update the tests, which gets us more coverage); probably not something tests should require

This would decouple podman testing from what users actually see (since we install the distro's containers-common package).

> Almost certainly the best option: modify the tests to be similar to the podman image trust show test above, which passes a --policypath option pointing to a test-specific policy file with fixed contents, instead of making assumptions about the surrounding environment. (A quick fix: Make that file with F34-like contents. A better fix: Make that file with more complex contents, closer to F35, and adjust expected output to match that. E.g. the existing https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/3c1d9910afaee21050ecbf058b5eddffc74c7abe/test/policy.json is a pretty good example policy, and using that for all the podman image trust show tests would be elegantly consistent.)

I agree this is probably the best option.  For reference, I've attached the F35 policy.json file copied from a CI VM.
[policy.json.txt](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/7391020/policy.json.txt)

If this fix will take a while to implement, I'm okay with opening an issue on it and marking the tests to skip, temporarily.

> Did the default contents of /etc/containers/policy.json change between F34 to F35

I believe this default file would have come from [containers-common-1-32.fc35-noarch (log)](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/6614426968129536?logs=package_versions#L6).  I looked, and didn't see any place where we replace this file at runtime (unless that test/suite does it - I didn't look).

> If this is explained by the distribution default config changing, there are about three ways to handle it:
>
> * Update the tests to expect the new system-wide default policy contents (but the tests would start failing on other systems / versions)

Damn.

> * Modify the system-wide OS configuration to have a specific policy.json content, e.g. the old one (or better, the new one and update the tests, which gets us more coverage); probably not something tests should require

This would decouple podman testing from what users actually see (since we install the distro's containers-common package).

> Almost certainly the best option: modify the tests to be similar to the podman image trust show test above, which passes a --policypath option pointing to a test-specific policy file with fixed contents, instead of making assumptions about the surrounding environment. (A quick fix: Make that file with F34-like contents. A better fix: Make that file with more complex contents, closer to F35, and adjust expected output to match that. E.g. the existing https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/3c1d9910afaee21050ecbf058b5eddffc74c7abe/test/policy.json is a pretty good example policy, and using that for all the podman image trust show tests would be elegantly consistent.)

I agree this is probably the best option.  For reference, I've attached the F35 policy.json file copied from a CI VM.
[policy.json.txt](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/7391020/policy.json.txt)

If this fix will take a while to implement, I'm okay with opening an issue on it, and skipping the tests temporarily.
Another idea, since F35 is still in beta:  Could we "fix" containers-common to remove the redhat stuff?

> Another idea, since F35 is still in beta: Could we "fix" containers-common to remove the redhat stuff?

(I’m honestly somewhat surprised that we ship these RH specifics in a Fedora package — OTOH it does have clear upsides.)

Having the tests cause force a restriction of what we can ship in production seems like a wrong trade-off in principle; tests should follow what the product does — and we do have a viable path to update the tests.

I could imagine a _very short-term_ workaround of changing the RPM during development only to unblock other work, but we can just skip the test as a similar class of workaround with much more local impact/downside.

> If this fix will take a while to implement, I'm okay with opening an issue on it, and skipping the tests temporarily.

To be explicit, would you prefer for me (or someone else) to work on updating the tests?

> To be explicit, would you prefer for me (or someone else) to work on updating the tests?

Yes please, I don't know this code any more that to be a danger to myself or others.  I've also got other failures unearthed by this PR that I need to reporo and open issues on.  Followed very shortly by the same thing again with new Ubuntu VM images :confounded:

> > To be explicit, would you prefer for me (or someone else) to work on updating the tests?
> 
> Yes please

Something vaguely like the new commits in #12060 — note that that code is absolutely untested at this point, the CI run will be the first run ever. I’ll continue working on that, for now just recording this to have a pointer.

>  I’ll continue working on that, for now just recording this to have a pointer.

Thanks @mtrmac

> I’ll continue working on that, for now just recording this to have a pointer.

@cevich the https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/12060 changes seem to work now; feel free to integrate this into your PR, or do you want me to drop the F35 parts and have it merged in Podman independently (and if so, can it be on top of the top of the `main` branch rather than the branch point of this F35 PR)?

@mtrmac great news!  I'll rebase it into this PR as soon as I can.  I'm having trouble rebuilding images this morning due to trouble in Ubuntu-land :confounded: 

Edit: Yes, please rebase #12060 on top of main, so I can rebase it in here.

@giuseppe did something change in cgroups or crun, that would [make the proc contents change like this (int podman fedora-35 root host logs)](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/6361378903556096/html/int-podman-fedora-35-root-host.log.html#t--podman-run-with-cgroups-disabled-runs-without-cgroups--1):

```
           Expected
               <string>: 0::/system.slice/google-startup-scripts.service/supervisor
           to equal
               <string>: 0::/system.slice/google-startup-scripts.service
```

We don't see this error in F34, so it must be something new.  Any ideas?

F35 "bindings" test hangs after 5-minutes (normally completes in about 10).  [Logs](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4631568376397824?logs=main#L286)  I tried adding collection of more logs/details, but apparently that's also subject to the task-timeout.  I can try wrapping the test itself in a timeout to help with that...but it's not going to fix the hang.

@jwhonce or @baude do you have any ideas?

> @giuseppe did something change in cgroups or crun, that would [make the proc contents change like this (int podman fedora-35 root host logs)](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/6361378903556096/html/int-podman-fedora-35-root-host.log.html#t--podman-run-with-cgroups-disabled-runs-without-cgroups--1):
> 
> ```
>            Expected
>                <string>: 0::/system.slice/google-startup-scripts.service/supervisor
>            to equal
>                <string>: 0::/system.slice/google-startup-scripts.service
> ```
> 
> We don't see this error in F34, so it must be something new. Any ideas?

there should not be any difference that can cause that issue :/
The `supervisor` bug is #11191

@edsantiago Q. for ya.  I'm trying to get more info. from the system why [the `Test Bindings` task](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/6470330748239872) is "hanging" after 5 minutes only to hit the cirrus-timeout after an hour.  I tried adding in [this `timeout` command](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/11795/files#diff-db575befc56ec85f83b824430b9b62e20eb19d08b351dc743db26848639896a6R87-R89), expecting the test to get killed after 30min.  But it doesn't seem to be doing anything at all.  Could the sub-shell and/or pipe into `logformatter` somehow be to blame?

@jwhonce I got it now so ginkgo times out the tests, and this allowed me to collect some more important details.  We're not running out of FDs, but [maybe are leaking mounts somewhere (log)](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4512494199767040?logs=df#L9) - or this is just a symptom of hanging in the middle of a build-test.  We also now have the pretty-looking annotated results, [showing it's consistently hanging in more/less the same place every time (logs)](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/4512494199767040/html/bindings-podman-fedora-35-root-host.log.html).  Is it expected that each step in the test should use a different socket?
@cevich I don't see where Cirrus is uploading the server logs from the run, can you provide me a link? TIA

> I don't see where Cirrus is uploading the server logs

It's in the task WebUI...that get's swamped by the failed/timeout test section `RUN MAIN` - shrink that down, and you'll see a bunch of sections below with logs.  e.g. [`df` section](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4512494199767040?logs=df#L9).

Note: F35 is running BTRFS now by default in their cloud images, so this is a significant change from F34.
Force-pushed to test with @giuseppe (now merged) #12120

[Example log of Test Bindings hanging](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4627523322511360?logs=main#L253) when trying to start a container

@jwhonce (reminder/I know you're feeling unwell) Have you had a chance to peek at this?

Fix for the hang is:
```diff
diff --git a/pkg/bindings/test/common_test.go b/pkg/bindings/test/common_test.go
index d996595bf..233666a48 100644
--- a/pkg/bindings/test/common_test.go
+++ b/pkg/bindings/test/common_test.go
@@ -151,7 +151,7 @@ func createTempDirInTempDir() (string, error) {
 }

 func (b *bindingTest) startAPIService() *gexec.Session {
-       cmd := []string{"--log-level=debug", "--events-backend=file", "system", "service", "--timeout=0", b.sock}
+       cmd := []string{"--log-level=debug", "system", "service", "--timeout=0", b.sock}
        session := b.runPodman(cmd)

        sock := strings.TrimPrefix(b.sock, "unix://")
```
I'm pretty upset at having wasted so much time on this. Something somewhere is not doing proper error checking, because podman is very clearly failing but this failure is not being caught:
```
time="2021-11-03T18:28:15-04:00" level=info msg="Request Failed(Internal Server Error): failed to obtain logs for Container '04e6c2bacbe6c275776c3fc93ed8de553cec02914f5eb6840b173141a50ff8a3': using --follow with the journald --log-driver but without the journald --events-backend (file) is not supported"
```
I'm also pretty upset at this new journald/file change that seems to be causing a lot of breakage everywhere. But I will call it a day now, and come back to revisit this in the morning.

And, may I suggest this change, to make it possible to see which tests are running and where things are hanging?
```diff
diff --git a/contrib/cirrus/runner.sh b/contrib/cirrus/runner.sh
index 8ef2a6e64..501e42208 100755
--- a/contrib/cirrus/runner.sh
+++ b/contrib/cirrus/runner.sh
@@ -84,7 +84,7 @@ function _run_bindings() {

     # Subshell needed so logformatter will write output in cwd; if it runs in
     # the subdir, .cirrus.yml will not find the html'ized log
-    (cd pkg/bindings/test && ginkgo -trace -noColor -debug  -r) |& logformatter
+    (cd pkg/bindings/test && ginkgo -trace -noColor -debug  -r -v) |& logformatter
 }

 function _run_docker-py() {
```
...both suggestions applied.

***Status***:  This PR is getting really close to being complete now.  I've identified one instability problem recently that needs addressing (via new VM images) relating to a random failure to expand the root filesystem on boot.
***Status***: My F34 disk-space fix made things worse, rebuilding images and will try again.

[Re-running `int podman fedora-35 rootless host` again](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/6026833767432192/html/int-podman-fedora-35-rootless-host.log.html#t--podman-run-with-cgroups-split--1), seeing "new" failures of `podman run with cgroups=split`:

```
[+0851s] Running: systemd-run --scope --user /var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/podman/bin/podman --storage-opt vfs.imagestore=/tmp/podman/imagecachedir --root /tmp/podman_test076439696/crio --runroot /tmp/podman_test076439696/crio-run --runtime crun --conmon /usr/bin/conmon --cni-config-dir /home/some30150dude/.config/cni/net.d --cgroup-manager systemd --tmpdir /tmp/podman_test076439696 --events-backend file --storage-driver vfs run --rm --cgroups=split quay.io/libpod/alpine:latest cat /proc/self/cgroup
[+0851s] Job failed. See "journalctl -xe" for details.
[+0851s] output: 
[+0851s] [AfterEach] Podman run
```

Sorry for a shotgun-mention, @edsantiago @giuseppe @mheon @rhatdan this is "brand-new" (as in wasn't failing like this two days ago).  It also appears to be very bad.  Searching [the journal](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/task/6026833767432192/logs/journal.log) for `podman_test076439696` finds this:

```
Nov 11 14:16:17 cirrus-task-6026833767432192 systemd[4466]: podman-84481.scope: Failed with result 'resources'.
Nov 11 14:16:17 cirrus-task-6026833767432192 systemd[4466]: Failed to start podman-84481.scope.
```

We have a `test/e2e/config.go` that the majority/all tests use to reference images.  I modified the quay builder to tag images based on the branch they came from.  So the trick then is, can we get `config.go` to use the compile-time `$CIRRUS_BRANCH` value for the tag.  That will make (for example) the release branches much more stable, since they're not using an ever-changing `latest` image.  I'm sure something similar can be done for the system-tests, if required.
I vote for completely eliminating the entire autobuild concept.

Note: I'm re-running the failed F34 tests to verify the 'podman search' failures are flakes.
They're flakes. One of the common ones. Every so often registry.redhat.io goes into a mode where it demands a login even for search. It's usually fixed in a few hours.

> They're flakes. One of the common ones. Every so often registry.redhat.io goes into a mode where it demands a login even for search. It's usually fixed in a few hours.

Yeah I've seen that before too, and guessed it's what we were seeing.  Thanks for confirming.

Hey, on that topic: What's your opinion on me adding a check to the `Ext. services` task (runs very early), to verify this? It would save people wasting time re-running due to a known issue we literally can't do anything about (AFAIK).  I'm on the fence about it vs getting "some" useful testing completed.

Well, it doesn't fit into the host/port model, so some hackery would be needed to try "podman search", which gets us into chicken/egg territory.

Thinking on it some more, though, I rarely see all `int` tests failing because of this. It's more typical to see just one or two. I don't know what that means about the nature of the flake, but from the perspective of CI, I think an early check would not be accurate enough: it could easily false-negative as well as false-positive.

Y'okay! Rebased/force-pushed "once last time" (note: this may not be the *real* "last time") :grin:
